The internal trade is conducted by means of markets and fairs, the latter of which, for the most part, had a religious origin, and still retain a religious character. The chief external trade is with Bombay westward. The principal im-

ports consist of cotton piece-goods, hardware, salt, cocoa-nuts, European liquors, tobacco, etc.; and the principal exports are raw cotton, grain, ghi, oil-seeds, and Indian piece-goods. Next in importance is the trade with the North-Western Provinces and Calcutta, the main imports being sugar from Mirzapur, piece-goods, indigo, jute bags, European liquors, etc.; and the exports, cotton for the mills at Cawnpore, lac, iron, grain, etc. With the Central Indian States a considerable traffic exists; but with the Nizam's dominions and Berar, and other parts of India, the trade is comparatively small. The Malwa opium, which passes through the province for export to China, now goes through Nimar to Bombay by rail.

The want of good means of communication, especially important in a land-locked region, has greatly retarded the progress of the Central Provinces. After the rains, the larger rivers become navigable, but the rocky barriers which occur in their channels restrict the use of this mode of transit. The making of roads, which may be said to date from the establishment of the British power, is rendered difficult by the nature of the country. Nagpur forms the centre of the road system. From that city branch off—the northern road, to Seoni and Jabalpur; the eastern line, by Bhandara and Raipur, to Sambalpur; the north-western, to Chhindwara; and the southern and south-western, to Chanda or Wardha. But besides these roads, numerous ancient tracks wind over hills and across the rocky beds of streams, along which the Banjaras drive their long trains of pack-bullocks. The Great Indian Peninsula Railway enters the Central Provinces near Burhanpur, and runs along the valley of the Narbada, passing Hosangabad, Narsinghpur, and Jabalpur, till it emerges from the north-east corner of the province, near Balihri. Starting from Bhusawal, a tributary line connects Wardha and Nagpur with the main railway, supplying communication with the coal-fields of Warora. A further branch has been constructed from Nagpur to Bilaspur, running thence east and north-east to join the East Indian Railway at Asansol; another line unites Bilaspur with Katni.

Nearly every form of land tenure found in India exists in the Central Provinces. Besides the estates of feudatory and of non-feudatory chiefs, known as zamindaris, the succession to which follows the law of primogeniture, what is termed the malguzari tenure prevails most widely. The estate, whether the property of one or many owners, is managed by a single proprietor, and the land is chiefly held by cultivators whose rents are thrown into a common stock. The profits are divided, or the losses made up, in proportion to the respective shares of the different proprietors.

BURMA.

The eastern-most province of British India, under the administration of a chief commissioner; bounded on the north and north-east by China, and tribes subordinate to China; on the north-west by Bengal, Assam, and the feudatory state of Manipur; and on the west and south by the sea. To the south-east lies the kingdom of

Siam. The total area, without the Shan States, is about 171,430 square miles, viz., Upper Burma, 83,473; Lower Burma, 87,957. The population of Lower Burma, according to the last census, was 4,658,627; that of Upper Burma was 2,946,933, giving a total for the province of 7,605,560.

The portion of Asia in which Burma is situated slopes from the central mountains towards the south; and the province is watered by five great streams, viz., the Irawadi and the Kyendwen, which unite their courses at 21^{\circ} 50' N. lat., the Sittaung or Paunglaung (Pounloun), the Salwin, and the Myit-nge. The first two rivers have their sources somewhere in the northern chain of mountains in the interior, one head stream of the Irawadi probably coming from Thibet; the Salwin has its source farther to the east in Thibet; the Sittaung in the hills to the south-east of Mandalay; and the Myit-nge, a large affluent of the Irawadi, drains the Shan States to the east of the capital. Except the last, which has a westerly direction, they all run in a southerly course to the Indian Ocean. The Irawadi and Salwin are large rivers, which, in the lower part of their course, overflow the flat country below their banks during the season of the rains, and higher up force their way through magnificent defiles. The former is navigable a considerable distance above Bhamo; but the latter is practically useless as a means of communication, owing to the frequent obstacles in its channel.

The northern portion of the province is, in the main, an upland territory, containing much rolling country, intersected by occasional hill ranges, and with a few isolated tracts of alluvial plain. Three chief ranges of hills traverse the southern portion from north to south, the Arakan Yoma, the Pegu Yoma, and the Paunglaung, of which the Tenasserim Hills, forming the boundary between our territory and Siam, may be regarded as a prolongation.

The country throughout the Delta is flat and uninteresting. Towards Prome, the valley of the Irawadi contracts, and the monotony of the plain is diversified by a wooded range of hills. The Salwin valley contains occasional harmonies of forest, crag, and mountain stream; but they bear the same relation to the wild sublimity of the Himalayas as the Trossachs to the Alps. On the other hand, the scenery in Tavoy and Mergui, and among the myriad islets which fringe the Tenasserim coast, is almost English in its verdure and repose. The forests of Burma abound in fine trees. Among these, teak holds a conspicuous place, some of the finest teak forests being in Pegu. Almost every description of timber known in India is produced in the forests, from which also an abundant supply is obtained of the varnish employed by the Shans and the Burmese in their manufacture of lacquered ware. Sticklac of an excellent quality is obtained in the woods, and rubber of late years has been largely exported.

Burma is rich in minerals. It produces gold, silver, copper, tin, lead, antimony, bismuth, amber, coal, petroleum, nitre, natron, salt, limestone, and marble, the jade or yu of the Chinese, sapphires, and other precious stones. Gold is found in small quantities at Shwe-gyin, in the sands of different rivers, and also towards the

Shan territory on the eastern frontier; but the demand is very much greater than the native supply. There are silver mines near the Chinese frontier, but they have not been recently worked. The mountainous districts of the Shan territory contain almost all the other metals; but they are but scantily exported, and the copper and tin seen in Mandalay are mostly imported from China. Iron is found in several places, and is wrought especially at Poppa, near a mountain of that name to the eastward of the old capital Pagan, and also at Myedu, north-west of Mandalay; but, owing to ignorance and the want of proper methods, about 30 or 40 per cent. of the metal is lost in the process. Large deposits of rich magnetic oxide, as yet untouched, exist in the ridges east of the capital near the banks of the navigable river Myit-nge, and the same district contains lime in great abundance, and of remarkable whiteness; while statuary marble, equal to the best Italian kind, is found about 15 miles north of Mandalay to the east of the Irawadi. Mines of amber are wrought, among other places, at Hukhong or Payendwen, near the sources of Kyen-dwen. Nitre, natron, and salt are found in various quarters. Sulphur also occurs in some places, as in the district of Sale-myo, and in the neighbourhood of the petroleum wells; but the quantity is comparatively small. Coal has been discovered in various places. Petroleum is found in Kyauk-hpyu, the Boronga Islands, Thayet-myo, and Ye-nangyaung; and this industry is rapidly developing. The precious stones produced are chiefly the sapphire and the ruby. They are found about 60 or 70 miles in a north-east direction from Mandalay, over an area of about 100 square miles.

In Lower Burma, agriculture is the main employment of the people, and it may be assumed that the production and distribution of rice occupies three-fifths of the whole population. Cotton, sesamum, and tobacco are also extensively grown; gardens and orchards are found near every village; but rice covers about six-sevenths of the total area under cultivation. The enormous foreign demand, and the large profits recently obtained, have greatly increased the cultivation of the cereal. The Burmese are content with a single annual crop, corresponding with the aman ropa of Bengal. It is sown in June, transplanted in September, and reaped about December or January. Their soil is lavish in its yield, requires little labour, and no artificial stimulus beyond the ash of the past year's stubble, which is burned down and worked into the land. Year after year, without a rest, the heavy rains and this primitive manure suffice to ensure an abundant harvest. The Irawadi valley and its delta furnish about three-fifths of the whole rice produce.

Upper Burma, though inferior in point of fertility to the low-lying tracts of Lower Burma, is far from unproductive. The chief crops are rice (of which the Burmese count 102 different sorts), maize, millet, wheat, various pulses, tobacco, cotton, sesamum, mustard, and indigo. The sugar-cane appears to have been long known to the Burmese; but, though the climate and soil are extremely favourable, it is not generally cultivated. A cheap and coarse sugar is obtained from the juice of the Palmyra palm, which abounds in the tract south of Mandalay. The cocoa nut and areca palms are

not common. The tea-plant, which is indigenous, is cultivated in the hills by some of the mountain tribes at the distance of about five days' journey, and by others in still greater perfection at the distance of about ten days' journey from Mandalay. It seems, however, to be another plant, probably the Elodendron persicum, which furnishes the principal ingredient in the hlapet, or pickled tea, that forms a favourite Burmese delicacy, and is an essential accompaniment to every social or ceremonial incident. Cotton is grown chiefly in the dry lands and climate of the upper districts. Indigo is indigenous, and is universally cultivated, but in a very rude manner; it is still more rudely manufactured, so as to be wholly unfit for exportation abroad.

The commercial prosperity of Lower Burma has more than kept pace with its rapidly-increasing population. Rangoon absorbs over 80 per cent. of the whole foreign trade of the province. The chief articles exported are rice, timber, cutch, hides, petroleum, and precious stones. The chief imports are piece-goods, silk, cotton and wool, cotton twist, gunny-bags, betel-nuts, liquors, tobacco, iron, mill machinery, and sugar.

The main commercial industries are those connected with the rice and timber trade. The indigenous manufactures of the country produce little beyond what is required for home consumption. Silk, lacquered ware, and gold and silver work, are among the most justly-admired of Burmese manufactures. The characteristics of Burmese art are vigour and novelty of design, but want of delicacy and finish in execution.

The dynasty which till recently reigned in Upper Burma was founded in the year 1752 A.D., by Alaungpaya, who, in early life a hunter, then a dacoit leader, established himself on the throne and extended his authority over the whole of Burma, including Arakan, Pegu, and Tenasserim, to the sea and the borders of Siam. The early relations of the house of Alaungpaya with the Indian government are matters of history. In the reign of Bagyidawpaya, the seventh king and fourth in descent from Alaungpaya, occurred the first war between England and Burma, terminated in 1826 by the treaty of Yandabo, whereby the provinces of Arakan and Tenasserim were ceded to the British. In the reign of Bagyidawpaya's nephew, Pagan Min, occurred the second Burmese war, at the conclusion of which, in December, 1852, Lord Dalhousie by proclamation annexed to the Indian Empire the province of Pegu, including the deltas of the Irawadi and the Sittaung, and the valleys of those rivers as far north as the parallel of latitude six miles north of the fort at Myede. Ten years later the province was organised, and a chief commissioner appointed. The territory which remained to the Burmese king was the country now known as Upper Burma. The pacification of the newly-acquired province of Pegu was a work of much labour, and was not satisfactorily completed till ten years after the annexation.

Almost immediately after the end of the second war, Pagan Min was deposed by his brother, thenceforward known as Mindon Min, or King Mindon. In 1867, a treaty was con-

cluded at Mandalay between the British and Burmese governments, providing for the mutual extradition of criminals, the free intercourse of traders, and the establishment of permanent diplomatic relations between the two countries. So long as Mindon Min lived, though he clung to the obsolete ceremonials to which he was accustomed, and thus debarred the British resident at Mandalay in his later years from access to his presence, there was no breach in the friendship between England and Burma.

In October, 1878, Mindon Min died, and was succeeded by his son, the Thebaw prince. The cruelties which marked the accession of the new monarch attracted more notice than on previous similar occasions. Indignation among Englishmen at the state of affairs in Mandalay, and resentment in the minds of the courtiers of the king on account of the attitude of the resident, combined to render imminent a breach of the friendly relations between the two countries. The immediate apprehension of war passed away; but throughout the year the tension continued, and in October, 1879, in view of the passive unfriendliness of the Burmese government, and the unsatisfactory nature of the position of the British resident in Mandalay, the government of India withdrew their representative from the Burmese court, and, as long as the native government continued to exist, no fresh agent was appointed.

These occurrences were sinister omens for the reign of King Thebaw. Nor did the progress of events afford prospect of the re-settlement of the relations between the Indian and Burmese governments. The actions of the latter soon gave abundant reason for complaint. British subjects, travellers and traders from Lower Burma, were subjected to insult and violence by local officials in Upper Burma, and representations to the central administration were often fruitless in obtaining redress. In contravention of the express terms of the treaty of 1867, monopolies were created to the detriment of the trade of both England and Burma. Owing to the weakness and corruption of the Burmese government, society became thoroughly disorganised, and the elements of disorder on our frontier became a standing menace to the peace of the British provinces. At the same time, the Burmese government showed a marked and persistent anxiety to enter into alliances with foreign powers, in such a manner and to such an extent as to give ground for apprehension that grave political trouble might hereafter result. While the Indian government was unrepresented at Mandalay, representatives of Italy and France were welcomed, and two separate embassies were sent to Europe, one under the guise of a merely commercial mission, for the purpose of contracting new and, if possible, close alliances with sundry European powers. Such was the state of affairs at the beginning of 1885. Early in that year, the Burmese government found or made reason for complaint against the Bombay-Burma Trading Corporation, a company of merchants, chiefly British subjects, who had extensive dealings in Upper Burma, and, without giving them reasonable opportunity of being heard, condemned them to pay a fine amounting to £230,000. Acting under the orders of the supreme government, the chief commissioner protested against this arbitrary

act of the Burmese authorities, and invited them to stay proceedings against the corporation, and to refer the matter to an arbitrator appointed by His Excellency the Viceroy. The reply of the Burmese foreign minister was that on no account should proceedings against the corporation be suspended. In view of this decisive and discourteous refusal to accept the mediation of the British government in a case where the interests of British subjects were largely involved—and bearing in mind the accumulated causes of dissatisfaction with the existing state of things—the government of India decided once for all to adjust the relations between the two countries. The chief commissioner was therefore instructed to send to the King of Burma an ultimatum requiring him to suspend action in the execution of the decree against the corporation, to receive at Mandalay an envoy from the Viceroy with a view to the settlement of the matter at issue, and, hereafter, to permit the residence at the capital of an agent of the Indian government, who should be received and treated with the respect due to the government which he represented. It was further intimated that the Burmese government would in future be required to regulate the external relations of the country in accordance with the advice of the government of India, and to afford facilities for opening up British trade with China. On the 6th November, a reply, amounting to an unconditional refusal of the terms, was received in Rangoon. On the 14th an expeditionary force crossed the frontier. On the 17th, Minhla, on the 23rd, Pagan, and on the 25th, Myingyan, were successively occupied. Except at Minhla, scarcely any resistance was encountered. Before the expeditionary force reached Ava, an envoy from the Burmese court arrived, and, after some negotiation, the unconditional surrender of the capital and of the royal family was arranged. On the 26th and 27th November, the forts at Ava and Sagaing were surrendered; and, on the 28th, General Prendergast and his force occupied Mandalay. The king and his two queens, with their mother, were at once sent down to Rangoon, whence the former were afterwards transferred to India, the latter to Tavoy. Simultaneously with the advance on Mandalay, operations were undertaken with success on the Thayetmyo and Toungoo frontiers.

Immediately after the occupation of Mandalay a provisional administration was constituted. All the members of the Hlutdaw, or great Council of State, professed themselves willing to continue to take part in the government. They were therefore retained in office, under the guidance of Colonel (afterwards Sir E. B.) Sladen, and the control of General (now Sir Harry) Prendergast. Theoretically there was to be no breach of continuity. The state council was to continue to discharge all its functions, and all civil officers, whether British or Burmese, were to work under its direction. The council began by issuing proclamations to this effect, and ordering Burmese officials to continue in the regular performance of their duties.

On the 1st January, 1886, by the Viceroy's proclamation, Upper Burma was declared to be part of Her Majesty's dominions, and placed under the direct administration of the governor-general.

The military occupation of Mandalay had been effected without the smallest difficulty, and in a manner which reflected credit on all concerned. To this, as was foreseen, succeeded a period of disorder and of straggling warfare which assumed dimensions somewhat, though not materially, exceeding what was anticipated. The same difficulties, however, were encountered upon the annexation of Lower Burma, and were not finally overcome for a period of ten years after the occupation of that province, although the general pacification of the country may be said to have been effected at the end of the third year. Before the rainy season of 1888, all the districts of Upper Burma, except two, namely, Sagaing and Minbu, had been pacified; and by the middle of 1889 all the large gangs of rebels that had given trouble in the plains were completely broken up and the remnants of once powerful bands had taken refuge in the inaccessible broken tracts which form a marked feature of the upper country.

One of the most urgent wants of Burma is land communication. There is abundance of waterway, however, throughout the delta all the year round, but the Sittang valley has no such advantages. The railway to Mandalay from Toungoo (the then terminus of the Sittang Valley line), for which a preliminary survey was sanctioned in November, 1886, was opened for traffic in March, 1889. Since the railway was opened the districts through which it runs have been the quietest in Upper Burma. The traffic on the line is already considerable. Another line is now under construction which is to connect Mandalay with Mogoung, and the section between the former city and Wuntho has been already completed. Large outlay has been incurred and still continues upon barracks, hospitals, roads, and necessary civil buildings.

NATIVE STATES.

That the territories of the Native States of India occupy no small proportion of the entire Indian peninsula and contain over one-fifth of the whole population of the country will be seen from the statistical table printed at page 180.

Many of the Feudatory States are governed almost independently by their own rulers, but they are all subject in a greater or less degree to supervision and guidance at the hands of the British Government. They may be divided into two main heads: (1) States holding direct relations with the Governor-General in Council; and (2) States holding relations primarily with the several Local Governments of India.

STATES UNDER THE DIRECT SUPERVISION OF THE GOVERNOR-GENERAL IN COUNCIL.

1. Hyderabad.—The Hyderabad State is roughly co-extensive with the Deccan or central plateau of Southern India and takes its name from its capital city. With the exception of the assigned districts known as Berar, a tract of some 17,000 square miles, the whole of this province is governed by the Nizam. The total area, including Berar, is about 100,416 square miles, and the population is estimated to be 14,434,531, about one-tenth being Muhammadans. The ruling

dynasty is of Turkoman origin, the name of the first sovereign being Chin Killich Khan, also known as Asaf Jah, who was appointed to be Viceroy of the Deccan with the title of Nizam-ul-Mulk in the year 1713. During the dissensions in the empire subsequent to the death of Aurungzeb, Asaf Jah succeeded in asserting his own independence. The Assigned Districts of Berar are administered by British officers in trust for the Nizam, and the government maintains out of their revenues an auxiliary force of four cavalry regiments, four field batteries of artillery, and six regiments of infantry. The raison d'être of this force was primarily that it might be of use to the Nizam, but it is available also under treaty for general service with British troops when required. The surplus revenue derived from Berar, after paying for the cost of the administration, is paid to the Nizam's government, and now amounts to about eight lakhs of rupees per annum.

2. Mysore.—The Mysore State is situated in Southern India between 11° 40' and 15° N. lat., and between 74° 40' and 78° 30' E. long., and is surrounded on all sides by British territory. Its total area is 27,936 square miles, and it contains a population of 4,943,604. The inhabitants are almost exclusively Hindus, who constitute more than 94 per cent. of the whole population. In early times Mysore was the principal seat of the Jains, who have left many interesting memorials of their occupation. The state has always been under Hindu rulers, except during the short interval caused by the usurpation of power during the 18th century by Haidar Ali and his son, Tipu Sultan. After the death of the latter, at the capture of Seringapatam in 1799, the English restored a representative of the ancient line in the person of Krishna Raj, son of Chama Raj of Arakotara. His subsequent misrule, when admitted to power on attaining his majority, led to the resumption of the administrative control of the province by the British government in the year 1831. The government, however, continued to be carried on in the name of the native prince, and at his death an adopted son was recognised as successor. This chief was duly installed on coming of age in 1881. The British chief commissioner thereupon handed over office to the native diwan, and a political resident was appointed to represent British interests.

3. Baroda.—This state includes all the territories of his Highness the Gaekwar. Its total area is 8,226 square miles, and it contains a population of 2,415,396 souls, the majority of whom (over 89 per cent.) are Hindus. The province of Gujarat was at one time included in the Mughal empire, but, in the early part of the seventeenth century, the Marhattas, under the leadership of Damaji Gaekwar, and afterwards of his son, Pilaji, succeeded in wresting all power out of the hands of the Mughal officers. From that time Baroda has remained continuously under the sway of the Gaekwar family, who held it in the first instance under the Marhatta Peshwa subject to a tributary payment, but afterwards threw off their allegiance and became feudatory to the British government under the guarantee of a treaty executed in the year 1817. The British government is represented at the court of the Baroda State by a political officer, called the governor-general's agen.

4. Kashmir and Jammu.—This native state includes, in addition to the district of Kashmir proper, Jammu, Poonch, and the governorships of Ladakh and Gilgit. The area is estimated to be 80,900 square miles. The population, according to the census of 1891, amounts to 2,543,952. Kashmir became part of the Mughal empire in 1586 A.D., but it was afterwards subjugated both by the Afghans and the Sikhs. At the time of the Sikh war the state was under the government of Gulab Singh, a Sikh officer, who ruled as a feudatory of the Maharaja of the Punjab. After the victory of the British forces in 1846, the possession of the kingdom was confirmed to Gulab Singh, under certain specified conditions of alliance and subordinate co-operation, by a separate treaty executed at Amritsar in the same year. A political resident is permanently stationed with the head-quarters of the Kashmir state, and represents the authority of the governor-general; without his concurrence no changes of importance can be carried through by the state council, of which the Maharaja is president. The ruling family is Hindu, but the greater number of the inhabitants of the country, considerably more than half, are Muhammadans, and chiefly of the Sunni sect.

5. Kalat.—The Khan of Kalat is head of the Baluchistan tribal chiefs whose territories are comprised under the following divisions:—Jhalawan, Sarawan, Makran, Las, and Kachh Gandava. These districts form what may be termed Kalati Baluchistan, and occupy an area of about 100,000 square miles. The inhabitants of the

country are either Brahuis or Baluchis, both being Muhammadans of the Sunni sect. The country is sparsely populated, the total number being roughly estimated at 350,000 persons only. It derives its chief importance from its position with regard to Afghanistan on the north-western frontier of British India. The Khan of Kalat is under a treaty obligation of subordinate co-operation with the British government. The governor-general's agent in Baluchistan conducts the relations between the government of India and the Khan, and exercises a general political supervision over the district.

6. Rajputana Agency.—Rajputana is the name of a great territorial circle, situated between 23° and 30° N. lat., and between 69° 30' and 78° 15' E. long. Within these limits are twenty native states, having each its own autonomy and separate chief, together with the British district of Ajmere-Merwara. Of the Native States, 17 are Rajput, 2 are Jat (Bhartpur and Dholpur), and 1 only is Muhammadan (Tonk). Their combined area is about 130,000 square miles, and they contain a population of over 12 millions. The chief administrative control of the British district is vested ex officio in the political officer, who holds the post of governor-general's agent for the supervision of the relations between the several native states of Rajputana and the government of India.

The following table, compiled chiefly from the returns taken in 1891, gives a detailed statement of the estimated area and population (including forest tribes) and the approximate revenues of the states in the Rajputana Agency:—

Name. Area
in square
miles.		 Population. Approximate
Revenue.
Rx.
Alwar ... .. 3,051 767,786 266,000

Bikanir ... .. 23,090 831,955 180,000
Bundi ... .. 2,225 295,675 80,000
Dholpur ... .. 1,156 279,890 93,000
Dungarpur ... .. 1,440 165,400 15,000
Jaipur ... .. 15,349 2,832,276 620,000
Jaisalmer ... .. 16,039 115,701 16,000
Jhalawar ... .. 3,043 343,601 150,000
Karauli ... .. 1,229 156,587 48,000
Kishengarh ... .. 874 125,516 36,000
Kotah ... .. 3,803 526,267 240,000
Lawa ... .. 19 3,360 500
Marwar or Jodhpur ... .. 37,445 2,521,727 416,000

7. Central India Agency.—Central India is the name given to the country occupied by the native states grouped together under the supervision of the political officer in charge of the Central India Agency. These states lie between 21° 24' and 26° 32' N. lat., and between 74° 0'

and 83° 0' E. long. The British districts of Jhansi and Lalitpur divide the Agency into two main divisions—Native Bundelkhand and Baghelkhand lying to the east, and Central India proper to the west. The total area covered is about 77,800 square miles, and the population

amounts to 10,318,812. The great majority of the people are Hindus. The principal states are six in number—Gwalior, Indore, Bhopal, Rewa, Dhar, and Jaora, of which two, Bhopal and Jaora, are Muhammadan, and the rest are Hindu. Besides these there are a multitude of petty states held by their rulers under the immediate guarantee of the British govern-

ment, but having feudal relations with one or other of the larger states. The total number of states amounts to 69, and the petty chiefs to 79—total, 148.

The list below gives the approximate size, population, and revenue of the six principal states above detailed:—

Name. Area. Population. Approximate Revenue.
Sq. m. Rx.
Gwalior ... .. 29,046 3,366,496 1,419,000
Indore ... .. 8,402 1,094,150 650,000
Bhopal ... .. 6,874 952,486 270,000
Rewa... .. 10,083 1,508,943 130,000
Dhar ... .. 1,740 169,474 80,000
Jaora ... .. 581 117,650 95,000

STATES UNDER THE LOCAL GOVERNMENTS AND ADMINISTRATIONS.

1. Under the Government of Madras.—The Madras Presidency includes five native states covering an area of 9,609 square miles, with a population of 3,700,622 souls. Approximate revenue, Rx. 1,000,000.

Of these states, Travancore and Cochin represent ancient Hindu dynasties. Pudukota is the inheritance of a chieftain called Tondiman. Banganapalli and Sandur, two petty states, of which the first is ruled by a Nawab, lie in the centre of two British districts. The principal states are:—

Name. Area. Population. Approximate Revenue.
Sq. m. Rx.
Cochin ... .. 1,362 722,906 175,000
Travancore ... .. 6,730 2,557,736 840,000

2. Under the Government of Bombay.—The native states in this presidency number about 370. Area, 69,045 square miles. Popula-

tion, 8,059,298. Revenue about Rx. 3,451,000. The list below gives details of the area, &c., of the more important states:—

Name. Area. Population. Approximate Revenue.
Sq. m. Rx.
Bhaunagar ... .. 2,860 467,282 350,000
Dhrrangadra... .. 1,156 103,754 78,000
Gondal ... .. 1,024 161,036 12,000
Idar ... .. 1,900 302,134 60,000
Junagarh ... .. 3,280 484,190 240,000
Khairpur ... .. 6,109 131,937 73,000
Kolhapur ... .. 2,816 913,131 331,000
Kutch... .. 6,500 558,415 180,000
Morvi... .. 821 105,335 10,000
Nawanagar ... .. 3,791 379,611 240,000
Palanpur ... .. 7,775 645,526 142,000
Rajpipla ... .. 1,514 171,771 77,000

3. Under the Government of Bengal there are twenty - nine states. Area, 35,834 square miles. Population, 3,296,379. Revenue, about Rx. 270,900.

These states may be divided into two groups: the first consisting of the petty states attached to the Orissa and Chota Nagpur divisions, 24 in number; and the other comprising the states of Kuch Behar to the north, and Hill Tipperah to the east, of Bengal. The inhabitants

of the first group are hillmen of Kolarian or Dravidian origin, and their condition is still very primitive. Kuch Behar is inhabited by a Mongoloid people, the Koch. The population of Kuch Behar consists of the Tibetan races of Lepcha and Bhōti, and the Nipālī tribe, Limbu. Hill Tipperah is the home of a Mongoloid race, partly drawn from the Eastern Himalayas, and allied to the Kachārī of Assam. The principal states are:—

Name. Area. Population. Approximate Revenue.
Kuch Behar... Sq. m. Rx.
Hill Tipperah 1,307 578,868 207,000
4,086 137,442 61,000

4. Under the Government of the N.-W.-P. and Oudh, the native territory comprises the following two states, the first being ruled by a

Muhammadan, and the second by a Hindu, dynasty.

Name. Area. Population. Approximate Revenue.
Rampur Sq. m. Rx.
Tehri (Garhwal) 945 551,249 300,000
4,164 241,242 23,000

Rampur is nearly equally divided between Muhammadans and Hindus. The inhabitants of Tehri are chiefly Hindus.

families in all India. Along the western half of the southern border lies the Muhammadan state of Bahawalpur. The remaining states, including the Sikh principalities of Patiala, Jind, Nabha, Kapurthala, Faridkot and Kalsia, and the Muhammadan chiefships of Maler Kotla, Pataudi, Loharu and Dujana, lie east of Lahore, and, with insignificant exceptions, occupy the centre of the eastern-plains of the province.

5. Under the Government of the Punjab there are 34 states, varying considerably in size and importance. Area, 38,299 square miles. Population, 4,263,280. Revenue, about Rx. 1,244,000.

The list below gives details of the area, population, and revenue of the more important states:—

The Punjab states may be grouped under three main classes. The hill states, 23 in number, lie among the Punjab Himalayas and are held by some of the most ancient Rajput

6. Under the Chief Commissioner of the Central Provinces are 15 states. Their total area is 29,435 square miles, with a population of

2,160,511 persons. Revenues, about Rx. 130,000. The inhabitants are chiefly Hindus and aborigines. The principal states are:—

Name. Area. Population. Approximate Revenue.
Bastar Sq. m. Rx.
Kalahandi 13,052 310,884 17,000
Patna... 3,745 326,295 12,000
2,399 332,197 9,000

7. Under the Chief Commissioner of Assam there is only one state—Manipur. Its area is estimated to be about 7,500 square miles, and it has a population of about 250,000, chiefly Kuki and semi-Hindu forest tribes. Revenue, about Rx. 28,000.

8. Under the Chief Commissioner of Burma there are a number of Shan states and other petty chiefships, the population of which was roughly estimated in 1890-91, when it was returned at 373,000.

LIST OF PARLIAMENTARY PAPERS RELATING TO INDIA ISSUED DURING 1894.

H.C. 43. Report (Decennial) on the Moral and Material Progress of India, 1891-92.
H.C. 199. Report on the Moral and Material Progress of India, 1892-93.
C. 7514. Report on Sanitary Measures in India, 1892-93.
C. 7513. Statistical Abstract, 1892-93.
H.C. 110. Home Accounts, 1892-93.
H.C. 109. Estimate of Revenue and Expenditure, 1893-94.
H.C. 92. Financial Statement, 1894-95.
C. 7465. Accounts and Estimates, 1894-95 : Explanatory Memorandum.
H.C. 55. Loans raised in England to 31st March, 1894.
H.C. 496. Loans raised in India to 30th September, 1893.
H.C. 190. Loans raised in India to 31st March, 1894.
C. 7280. Trade of British India, 1888-89 to 1892-93.
Lds. 143. Tariff Bill, 1894 : Discussion and Dissents.
Lds. 143 I. Financial Statement, 1894-95 : Proceedings.
H.C. 217. British India : Net Income and Expenditure, 1883-84 to 1892-93.
H.C. 7. Currency and Sale of Bills on India.
C. 7253. Sale of Bills on India : Correspondence.
H.C. 475. Silver : Imports and Exports, &c., 1890-93.
C. 7313. Opium Commission : First Report.
C. 7397. Ditto ditto Vol. II. : Evidence.
C. 7419. Ditto ditto Vol. III. : Ditto.
C. 7471. Ditto ditto Vol. IV. : Ditto.
C. 7473. Ditto ditto Vol. V. : Ditto.
H.C. 251. Opium : Bengal Government Resolution, 20th April, 1894.
H.C. 86. Legislative Councils : Regulations, &c.
H.C. 20. Army Expenditure, 1875 to 1892-93.
H.C. 509. Army : Average Numbers in Hospital for Venereal Diseases, 1870-92.
H.C. 538. Religious Disturbances.
C. 7281. Benares Religious Buildings, Rating of.
H.C. 205. Behar Cadastral Survey : Further Correspondence.
C. 7378. Indian Civil Service : Simultaneous Examinations.
H.C. 28. East India Pensions.
H.C. 281. India Pensions exceeding Rs. 5,000 per annum.
H.C. 108. Factory Inspection : Reports.
C. 7456. Baladhan Murder Case.
C. 7441. Grain Elevators, Introduction of.
C. 7440. Impurity of Indian Wheat : Papers, 1885-90.

ACT RELATING TO INDIA PASSED DURING 1894.

Indian Railways Companies : Payment of Interest out of Capital.

LIST OF THE MORE IMPORTANT INDIAN GOVERNMENT PUBLICATIONS
ISSUED DURING 1894.

THE FOLLOWING HAVE BEEN ADDED TO THE STOCK OF BOOKS ON SALE AT THE INDIA OFFICE.

Finance and Revenue Accounts of the Government of India, 1892-93. (2s. 6d.) The Administration of the Marquess of Lansdowne, 1888-94. By G. W. Forrest, B.A. (2s. 6d.)
Annals of the Royal Botanic Gardens, Calcutta. Vol. IV. (2l. 4s. 4d.). Punjab Customary Law, Vol. XII. (6d.)
Progress of the Madras Presidency. By S. Srinivasa Raghavaiyangar. (1s. 6d.) Destruction of Beer Casks by a Boring Beetle. By W. F. H. Blandford. (6d.)
Bombay Gazetteer, Vol. 26, parts 1 and 2. (5s. each.)

IN ADDITION TO THE USUAL PROVINCIAL AND DEPARTMENTAL REPORTS, THE FOLLOWING WORKS OF GENERAL INTEREST HAVE BEEN PUBLISHED IN INDIA.

Ajmere Code. Revised Edition. (Rs. 3.)

Report on Horse Surra, Vol. I. By Alfred Lingard, M.B., M.S. (Rs. 10.)

Epigraphia Indica, Parts 14 and 15. (Rs. 3 each.)

Prices and Wages in India. Eleventh Issue. (R. 1-8.)

Review of the Trade of India, 1893-94. (8a.)

Records of the Geological Survey, Vol. XXVI., Part 4; XXVII., Parts 1 to 3. (R. 1 per part or Rs. 2 per year of 4 parts.)

Indian Museum Notes. Vol. II., No. 7 (4a.). Vol. III., No. 3 (8a.)

Meteorological Memoirs. Vol. V., Part 4 (R. 1); VI., Part 1 (Rs. 2).

Archaeological Survey: The Bower M.S., Part II., Fasc. 1. By A. F. R. Hoernle, Ph. D. (Rs. 7.)

Notes on a Tour in Burma, 1892. By F. O. Oertel. (Rs. 16.)

Punjab District Gazettes. 2nd editions. Gujrat. (Rs. 3-2.)

Manual of the Administration of the Madras Presidency, Vol. III. (Rs. 10.)

THE FOLLOWING ARE THE PRINCIPAL MAPS ADDED TO THE SALE STOCK AT THE INDIA OFFICE.

(Except where otherwise stated, the maps are on one sheet. The figures in italics denote the number of miles to the inch.)

General Maps.—India, general map, without hills, 128 (3s. 6d.); India, shewing railways, 96 (4s.); India, ditto, with hills shaded, 64 (four sheets, 9s.); India, shewing area under cotton cultivation, 64 (two sheets, 13s.); India, shewing area under rice cultivation, 64 (two sheets, 10s.); India, shewing area under sugarcane cultivation, 64 (two sheets, 9s.); Assam, 8 (eight sheets, 2s. each); ditto, 16 (4s.); Bengal, 80 (6d.); ditto, 32 (3s.); ditto, 16 (two sheets, 5s.); Bombay, 32 (3s.); Hyderabad and Berar, 16 (two sheets, 4s.); Mysore and Coorg (skeleton), 16 (3s.); N.-W. Provinces and Oudh, 32 (1s.); Punjab and surrounding Countries (skeleton), 32 (1s. 6d.); Sind (without hills), 16 (3s.); Sind, &c., 2 (3s.); Upper Burma, 64 (6d.); ditto, 16 (two sheets, 6s.).

Divisional Maps.—Allahabad, 4 (four sheets, 4s.); ditto, 4 (two sheets, 4s.); Benares, 4 (two sheets, 4s.); Pegu, sheets 1 and 3, 4 (2s. each).

District Maps.—Agra, 8 (6d.); Akola, 8 (6d.); Amraoti, 8 (6d.); Amritsar, 2 (two sheets, 4s.); Banda, 8 (6d.); Bara Banki, 8 (6d.); Bareilly, 8 (6d.); Basim, 8 (6d.); Bhagalpur, 4 (2s. 6d.);

Bhamo, 8 (2s. 6d.); Bijnor, 8 (6d.); Budaun, 8 (6d.); Buldana, 8 (6d.); Cachar, 8 (6d.); Cawnpore, 8 (6d.); Darjeeling, 4 (2s.); Ellichpur, 8 (6d.); Faridpur, 8 (6d.); Farukhabad, 8 (6d.); Fatehpur, 8 (6d.); Fyzabad, 8 (6d.); Goalpara, 4 (3s.); ditto, 8 (6d.); Hardoi, 8 (6d.); Hugli, 4 (2s.); Katha, 4 (3s.); Kheri, 8 (6d.); Lohardaga, 16 (6d.); Murshidabad, 8 (6d.); Muzaffarpur, 8 (6d.); Nadia, 4 (2s.); Naga Hills, 4 (4s.); Palamau, 4 (3s.); Puri, 8 (6d.); Rai Bareli, 8 (6d.); Ruby Mines, 4 (2s. 6d.); Sitapur, 8 (6d.); Sylhet, 8 (6d.); Wun, 8 (6d.).

Indian Atlas Sheets.—4, Nos. 30, 47, 73, 76, 79, 103, 104, 113 (4s. each); I N.E., 8 N.E., 9 S.E., 12 N.E., 22 N.W., 23 N.E., 25 S.E., 35 N.E., 61 N.W., 67 S.E., 69 S.E., 86 S.W., 87 S.E., 90 N.W., 91 N.E., 92 N.E., 105 N.E., 126 N.W., 129 S.W., 130 N.E., N.W., 131 S.W. (1s. 6d.) each).

Miscellaneous.—Burma (formerly North-Eastern and South-Eastern Frontier series), 4 and 8, sheets 1, I N.E., S.W., 3, 4 N.E., S.E., S.W., 7, 15, 22, 23 N.W., S.W. (3s. per sheet); Aden, 4 (3s.); Route Map for W. Himalayas, &c., 32 (4s.); Mandalay City, 1 (1s.); Indochina, 32 (two sheets, 6s.).

NOTE.—Particulars as to the above publications may be obtained on application to the Registrar and Superintendent of Records, India Office, or to any of the following

AGENTS TO THE INDIA OFFICE FOR THE SALE OF GOVERNMENT PUBLICATIONS.

For Books and Maps.

Messrs. W. H. ALLEN and Co., 13, Waterloo Place, S.W.

Mr. E. A. ARNOLD, 37, Bedford Street, Strand, W.C.

Messrs. A. CONSTABLE and Co., 14, Parliament Street, S.W.

Messrs. LUZAC and Co., 46, Great Russell Street, W.C.

Messrs. KEGAN PAUL, TRENCH, TRÜBNER and Co., Paternoster House, Charing Cross Road, W.C.

Mr. B. QUARITCH, 15, Piccadilly, W.

For Maps Only.

Mr. E. STANFORD, 26, Cockspur Street, Charing Cross, S.W.

Messrs. H. S. KING and Co., 65, Cornhill, E.C.

THE INDIA OFFICE LIST.
RECORD OF SERVICES.

secretary to Government of Bombay, revenue, financial, and General departments Oct. 1865; acting collector of Bombay and superintending officer of stamps and statutory March 1881; from April 1881 served as assistant collector of revenue; collector, and acting junior collector of revenue; member of the mixed commission at Goa, Dec. 1881; deputy municipal commissioner, July 1885; deputy collector of sale revenue, Aug. 1885; acting junior collector and magistrate, April 1887; municipal commissioner for the city of Bombay April 1889; acting collector of revenue, revenue, financial, and General departments Oct. 1889.

ABBOTT, EVARNE ROBERT, Indian C.S. (P.M.-lab).—Educated at Ball College and Balliol College, Oxford; appointed after open competition examination of 1867; arrived, 26th October 1869, and served in the Punjab as assistant commissioner.
ABBOTT, HENRY HAYNE, Lieutenant-Colonel, I.C.S., Forestry Department, Government of India.—First commission, June 1868; arrived in India Nov. 1868; served with the roadfoot; served as assistant resident. from Oct. 2037 to 2038 served as

THE INDIA OFFICE LIST.

RECORD OF SERVICES.

ABBOTT, EVELYN ROBINS, Indian C.S. (Punjab).—Educated at Bath College, and Balliol College, Oxford; appointed after open competitive examination of 1891; arrived, 30th October, 1893, and served in the Punjab as assistant commissioner.

ABBOTT, HENRY BYAM, Lieutenant-Colonel, I.S.C., Foreign Department, Government of India.—First commission, June, 1861; arrived in India, Nov., 1861; served with the 100th foot; served as assistant resident, Aden, 1865 to 1869; from Oct., 1869, to Feb., 1874, served as assistant agent, Rajputana, and acted as magistrate of Abu, and assistant general superintendent, thagi and dakaiti department, 1872-73; officiating settlement officer, Ulwar, 1874-75; officiating political agent, Eastern States, Rajputana, 1875; political assistant, Manpura, and deputy Bheel agent, 1875-76; on special duty at Jhallowar, 1876-77; superintendent of Jhallowar, from March, 1876; graded as additional political agent, 1st class, Dec., 1888; officiating resident at Gwalior, July, 1890; and at Meywar, Oct., 1890; political agent, Ulwar, Jan., 1891; officiating resident, Jeypur, May, 1891; on special duty in Central India, Jan., 1892; resident, Western States of Rajputana, April, 1892.

ABBOTT, HERBERT EDWARD STACY, Captain, R.E., India Public Works Department.—Joined the service, 16th Sept., 1878, and was posted to the Punjab; on field service during Afghan war, 1880-81 (medal); executive engineer, Jan., 1885; field engineer in Hazara expedition, 1888 (medal and clasp); and in Miranzai expedition, 1891 (clasp); under-secretary to Punjab government, public works department, May, 1893.

ABDUL GHAFUR KHAN OF ZAIDA, Punjab Commission.—Appointed probationer under native civil service rules, 14th Feb., 1889; assistant commissioner, Dec., 1891.

ABRAHAMS, BARNETT LIONEL, B.A., Financial Department, India Office.—Educated at the City of London School and Balliol College, Oxford (scholar, 1888-92; Jenkyns exhibitioner, 1892; Arnold prize, 1894); junior clerk, India office, 21st Feb., 1893; précis writer, Dec., 1894.

ABU TURAB KHAN, Agha, Statutory C.S. (N.W. Provinces and Oudh).—Appointed by the government of India a probationary assistant magistrate and collector under the statutory civil service rules, 16th April, 1889; confirmed, 8th Aug., 1892.

ACWORTH, HARRY ARBUTHNOT, B.L., Indian C.S. (Bombay).—Educated at Brighton College; appointed to the Bombay civil service after open competitive examination of 1868; arrived in India, 31st Oct., 1870, and served as assistant collector and magistrate, acting collector of stamp revenue and political agent, and acting collector and magistrate; acting under

secretary to government of Bombay, revenue, financial, and general departments, Oct., 1879; acting collector of Bombay and superintendent of stamps and stationery, March, 1881; from April, 1881, served as assistant collector, deputy collector, and acting junior collector of salt revenue; member of the mixed commission at Goa, Dec., 1884; deputy municipal commissioner, July, 1885; deputy collector of salt revenue, Aug., 1885; acting junior collector and magistrate, Aug., 1887; municipal commissioner for city of Bombay, April, 1890; editor of the Bombay Revenue Handbook, 3rd edition, and author of 'Ballads of the Marathas,' 1894.

ADAMS, GEORGE, Indian C.S. (North-Western Provinces).—Educated at Christ's Hospital; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1860; arrived in India, 30th Dec., 1861; from April, 1863, served in the N.W. Provinces as assistant magistrate and collector; deputy commissioner, Lalitpur, in the Jhansi commission, April, 1877; officiating commissioner, Jhansi, in 1878 and 1880; magistrate and collector, Nov., 1883; officiating commissioner, Sitapur, July, 1888; commissioner in Oudh, Nov., 1889; commissioner and governor-general's agent, Benares, Aug., 1890; commissioner, Lucknow, April, 1893; junior member, board of revenue, April, 1894; also member of provincial legislative council, June, 1894.

ADAMS, JOHN, Bombay Public Works Department.—Joined the service, 13th May, 1869; assistant engineer, Nov., 1871; executive engineer, Sept., 1881; architectural executive engineer and surveyor, Jan., 1882; is a fellow of Bombay university.

ADAMSON, CHARLES HENRY ELLISON, C.I.E., Lieut.-Col., I.S.C., Burma Commission.—First commission, royal artillery, Jan., 1868; served with 5th brigade at Kirkee and Rangoon till 1870; admitted to the Madras staff corps, 1870; joined the Burma commission, Dec., 1871, and served as assistant magistrate, assistant commissioner, and officiating magistrate, and president of municipal committee, Rangoon; deputy commissioner, June, 1885; C.I.E., May, 1889; commissioner, Jan., 1892.

ADAMSON, HARVEY, M.A., Indian C.S. (Burma).—Educated at the Gymnasium, Old Aberdeen, and King's College, Aberdeen; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1875; arrived at Rangoon, 17th Dec., 1877; served as assistant commissioner, officiating deputy commissioner, and settlement officer; 1st grade settlement officer, June, 1886; deputy commissioner, April, 1888; acted as commissioner in 1888, and from May, 1894.

ADDIS, DAVID FOULIS, M.A., Indian C.S. (North-Western Provinces).—Educated at the Universities of Glasgow and Edinburgh; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1870; arrived in India, 14th Nov., 1872, and served in the N.W. Provinces as assistant magistrate and collector, joint magistrate, assistant settlement officer, and officiating magistrate and collector; joint magistrate, 2nd grade, Dec., 1883; 1st grade, August, 1887; deputy commissioner, Jan., 1890; magistrate and collector, July, 1890.

ADDIS, HENRY BAWN, India Public Works Department.—Joined the service, 25th Jan., 1871, as assistant engineer, and was employed on the Neemuch, W. Rajputana and other railways; executive engineer, April, 1878; from Nov., 1887, was engaged on various railway surveys; services lent to Assam-Bengal railway company, April, 1892.

AGNEW, PATRICK DALREAGLE, Indian C.S. (Punjab).—Educated at Bedford Grammar School and Balliol College, Oxford; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competition of 1887; arrived in India, 1st Dec., 1889, and served in the Punjab as assistant commissioner, and assistant settlement officer; acted as junior secretary to financial commissioners, Oct. to Dec., 1892.

AGNEW, WILLIAM FISCHER, B.L., Judicial Department, Burma.—Reporter for Indian law reports, high court, Calcutta, from Feb., 1877; officiated as editor in 1879, 1882, and 1883; law lecturer, Presidency college, 1879; advocate, high court, Calcutta, 1879; recorder of Rangoon, March, 1884; officiating judge, high court, Calcutta in 1885 and 1886; author of 'Law of Patent,' 'The Statute of Frauds,' 'The Law of Trusts in India,' 'The Criminal Procedure Code,' and 'Index to Indian Acts.'

AHSANUDDIN AHMAD, B.L., Statutory C.S. (Bengal).—Appointed assistant magistrate and collector on probation under the native civil service rules, 3rd Jan., 1882; confirmed in the statutory civil service, June, 1887; joint magistrate and deputy collector, March, 1893.

AIKMAN, ROBERT SMITH, M.A., Indian C.S. (North-Western Provinces).—Educated at Madras College, St. Andrew's, and St. Andrew's University; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1865; arrived in India, 22nd Nov., 1867, and served in the N.W. Provinces as assistant magistrate and collector, and officiating magistrate and collector; transferred temporarily to Bengal on famine relief duty, Feb. to Oct., 1874; promoted to joint magistrate and deputy collector, Jan., 1879; from March, 1884, served in the judicial department, and was appointed judge, 3rd grade, Oct., 1886, and 2nd grade, March, 1890; officiating puisne judge, high court, Nov., 1892.

AINSLIE, WILLIAM, late Bengal C.S.—Appointed to the Bengal civil service from Haileybury College, 1845; arrived in India, 7th Nov., 1845; served in the Bengal revenue and judicial departments in the grades of assistant, and joint magistrate and deputy collector in Cuttack; and was specially employed in 1850-51 and 1851-52 in the suppression of Meriah sacrifices in the Khond districts; magistrate of Rungpore, July, 1852; railway commissioner, East Indian railway, Jan., 1856, to March, 1857; magistrate and

collector, Bhagulpore, March, 1857; railway commissioner, Eastern Bengal railway, 1858, and subsequently of the South-Eastern railway also; from Aug., 1863, served in the judicial department, and was appointed civil and sessions judge of Patna, Jan., 1864; president, tea commission, Nov., 1867, to April, 1868; officiated as judge of the high court, Calcutta, from Nov., 1870, and was confirmed in that appointment, May, 1873; retired, April, 1880.

AITCHISON, SIR CHARLES UMPHERSTON, K.C.S.I., C.I.E., M.A., LL.D., D.C.L., late Bengal C.S.—Educated at the University of Edinburgh; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1855; arrived in India, 24th Sept., 1856, and served in the N.W. Provinces as assistant commissioner and magistrate; transferred to the Punjab, May, 1857; personal assistant to judicial commissioner, July, 1858; officiating under secretary, government of India, foreign department, Nov., 1859; deputy-commissioner, Punjab, Jan., 1865; officiating commissioner, Lahore, Aug., 1867; officiating secretary to government, Punjab, Nov., 1867; from April, 1868, served in the foreign department, government of India, as officiating secretary, and was confirmed in this appointment, Aug., 1870; on special duty in thagi and dakaiti department, Oct., 1877; chief commissioner, British Burma, 31st March, 1878; provisional member, governor-general's council, July, 1880; lieutenant-governor, Punjab, 4th April, 1882, to April, 1887; member, governor-general's council, 2nd April, 1887, from which date he resigned the civil service; resigned his seat as member of governor-general's council, 17th Nov., 1888; has published 'Manual of the Criminal Law of the Punjab,' 1860; 'Treaties, Engagements, and Sanads relating to India and the Neighbouring Countries'; and 'The Native States of India.'

ALCOCK, JOHN BACKHOUSE, Indian C.S. (Bombay).—Appointed to the Bombay civil service after open competitive examination of 1874; arrived in India, 10th Nov., 1876, and served as assistant collector and magistrate, acting cantonment magistrate, and railway magistrate; from Jan., 1884, served as assistant judge and sessions judge, and joint judge and sessions judge; 3rd grade judge and sessions judge, March, 1894.

ALEXANDER, ERNEST BRUCE, Indian C.S. (North-Western Provinces).—Educated at Repton; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1869; arrived in India, 1st Nov., 1871, and served in the N.W. Provinces as assistant magistrate and collector; from Nov., 1874, to March, 1881, served as assistant settlement officer and officiating settlement officer in Fatehpur and Moradabad, and was appointed junior secretary to the board of revenue in addition to his other duties in 1877, and was placed on special duty under the orders of the board, Feb., 1879; served subsequently as assistant magistrate, and joint magistrate and deputy collector, and acted for various periods as small cause judge, and magistrate and collector; joint magistrate, 1st grade, Sept., 1885; deputy commissioner, June, 1889; magistrate and collector, Oct., 1889; in 1894 acted as secretary to government, judicial department; author of 'Gazetteer of Gorakhpur.'

ALEXANDER, JAMES WILLIAM, Public Works Department, Government of India.—Joined the

service, June, 1865; assistant engineer, Dec., 1866; assistant secretary to government of India, public works department, Jan., 1868; executive engineer, March, 1870; served in various divisions, and from Nov., 1886, to May, 1887, had charge of the Rohilkhand executive division; superintending engineer, Feb., 1890; in charge of first circle, Oct., 1893.

ALEXANDER, Moses, Store Department, India Office.—Appointed 2nd class clerk, 3rd March, 1888.

ALEXANDER, NATHANIEL STUART, late Bengal C.S.—Appointed from Haileybury College; arrived in India, 10th July, 1858; from Oct., 1859, served in the Bengal revenue and judicial departments, and was appointed magistrate and collector, 1st grade, Jan., 1879; commissioner, Dacca division, Aug., 1884; commissioner, Bhagulpore, Dec., 1885; and of Burdwan, Aug., 1887; retired, March, 1890.

ALISON, SIR ARCHIBALD, Bart., G.C.B., General, Member of the Council of India.—1st commission, ensign, 72nd foot, 3rd Nov., 1846; captain, Nov., 1853; major unattached, Dec., 1856; served in the Crimean campaign of 1855, and was present at siege of Sevastopol (medal and clasp and Turkish medal); served in the Indian mutiny, and was military secretary to the commander-in-chief, Aug., 1857, to March, 1858; was present at relief of Lucknow (wounded—arm lost, medal and clasp, brevet of lieutenant-colonel and C.B.); in staff employment, adjutant general's department at headquarters, and in the south western district, and at Aldershot, 1862 to 1873; served in the Ashantee war of 1873-4 as brigadier-general of European brigade, and was present at the battle of Amoafu, capture of Bequah, action of Ordashu, and taking of Coomassie; received thanks of both houses of parliament, medal with clasp, and K.C.B.; deputy adjutant general, headquarters, Ireland, Oct., 1874, to Dec., 1877; commandant, staff college, Jan.-April, 1878; deputy quartermaster-general, intelligence branch, May, 1878, to Aug., 1882; served in the Egyptian expedition of 1882 as major-general in command of troops at Alexandria, and on the Mahmoudiyeh canal, and in command of 3rd brigade, 2nd division, at battle of Tel-el-Kebir; received thanks of both houses of parliament, medal with clasp, bronze star, and 2nd class Osmanieh; promoted lieutenant-general for distinguished service, Nov., 1882; commanding division, Aldershot, Aug., 1883, to Feb., 1885; and again, Oct., 1885; adjutant-general to the forces, Feb.-Sept., 1885; G.C.B., June, 1887; appointed member of the council of India, Jan., 1889; general, Feb., 1889.

ALLEN, BASIL COPLESTON, B.A., Indian C.S. (Assam).—Educated at Haileybury College, and Corpus Christi College, Oxford; appointed after open competitive examination of 1892; arrived in India, 14th Dec., 1893, and served in Assam as assistant commissioner.

ALLEN, CHARLES FRANCIS EGERTON, B.A., M.P., late Judicial Department, Burma.—Called to the bar (Inner Temple), 1871; admitted advocate of high court, Calcutta, Nov., 1873; law examiner, Calcutta University, Dec., 1874, and again in 1875 and 1876; acting judge of the Calcutta court of small causes, 1875-76; lecturer on English law in the Presidency College, Calcutta, Feb., 1876, and professor of law (tempo-

rary), May, 1877; served as government advocate, Rangoon, and acted as recorder of Rangoon for various periods; M.P. for Pembroke District, 1892.

ALLEN, CHARLES GEORGE HILLERSDEN, Indian C.S. (Bengal).—Educated at Haileybury and Balliol College, Oxford; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1883; arrived in India, 16th Dec., 1885; assistant magistrate and collector, Jan., 1886; settlement officer, Chittagong, July, 1891; joint magistrate and deputy collector, Jan., 1893.

ALLEN, DAVID BIRD, Indian C.S. (Bengal).—Educated at Eton, and St. John's College, Cambridge; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1871; arrived in India, 5th Nov., 1873; served in revenue and judicial departments, Bengal, as assistant magistrate and collector, assistant to director-general of statistics, and officiating joint magistrate and deputy collector, and under the director of the agricultural department, May, 1885; joint magistrate and deputy collector, Sept., 1887; magistrate and collector, Aug., 1890.

ALLEN, JEFFERY JULIAN, M.I.E.E., India Telegraph Department.—Joined the service as assistant superintendent, 26th Nov., 1868; superintendent, Aug., 1881; on special duty, Jan., 1887; in charge, electrician's office, Calcutta, April, 1891; in charge, check office, June, 1894.

ALLEN, PERCY UNDERHILL, B.A., Indian C.S. (North-Western Provinces).—Educated at University College School, Cheltenham College, and Magdalen College, Oxford; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competition of 1885; arrived in India, 21st Nov., 1888, and served in the North-Western Provinces as assistant magistrate and collector; joint magistrate, April, 1892.

ALLEN, THOMAS TAYLOR, late Indian C.S. (Bengal).—Educated at Queen's College, Cork; appointed to Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1859; arrived in India, 7th Feb., 1861; from Nov., 1861, served in the Bengal revenue and judicial departments, and was appointed joint magistrate and deputy collector, April, 1866; district and sessions judge, March, 1875; lieutenant-governor's council, Jan., 1881, and again, Jan. to Dec., 1883, Nov., 1886, Dec., 1888, Dec., 1890, and Dec., 1892; superintendent and remembrancer of legal affairs, Feb., 1883; retired, Nov., 1894.

ALLEN, WILLIAM, late Bombay C.S.—Educated at King's College School, and St. Catherine's College, Cambridge; appointed to the Bombay civil service after open competitive examination of 1866; arrived in India, 10th Oct., 1868, and served as assistant collector and magistrate, acting railway magistrate, acting 1st class deputy collector in Sind, acting assistant conservator of forests, and acting collector and magistrate and political agent; 1st assistant collector, Dec., 1882; junior collector and magistrate, Nov., 1889; senior collector and magistrate, Nov., 1891; resigned, Sept., 1893.

ALLISON, HAZLETT, M.D., Surgeon-Lieut.-Col., Madras Medical Department.—Joined the service as surgeon, Sept., 1873; arrived in India, 29th April, 1874, and served on military duty from July, 1874; acted as fort surgeon, Fort St. George, medical inspector of seamen, and professor of anatomy in medical college at various periods;

fort surgeon, Fort St. George, with port and marine duties, professor of anatomy in the medical college, and medical inspector of seamen, Jan., 1883; in 1890 acted as superintendent, ophthalmic hospital, and in 1892 as chemical examiner and professor of chemistry; fellow of Madras university, Aug., 1892.

AMBICA CHARANSEN, M.A., Statutory C.S. (Bengal).—Appointed under the native civil service rules assistant magistrate and collector, 21st Jan., 1884, on probation; on duty under the director of the agricultural department, May, 1885; confirmed in the statutory civil service, 19th Feb., 1886; joint magistrate and deputy collector, Jan., 1892.

AMEER ALI, SYED, M.A., C.I.E., B.L., Bengal Judicial Department.—Educated at Calcutta University; called to the bar (Inner Temple), 1873; presidency and chief presidency magistrate, 1878-81; member of the Bengal legislative council, 1878-79, and 1881-83; member of the supreme legislative council, 1884-85; Tagore law professor at Calcutta University, 1884; C.I.E., 1887; officiating judge of the high court at Calcutta, 2nd Jan., 1890; confirmed, 9th April, 1890; author of the 'Tagore Law Lectures for 1884.'

AMEER HOSSEIN, SYED, C.I.E., Nawab, Bengal Judicial Department.—Joined the service as officiating deputy magistrate and deputy collector, 29th Aug., 1864; confirmed, 5th Feb., 1867; member of council of lieutenant-governor, April, 1878; officiated as presidency magistrate, Calcutta, for various periods, and was confirmed in that appointment Nov., 1888; member of central examination committee, April, 1886; additional member of governor-general's council, Dec., 1886, and again Dec., 1888; C.I.E., Jan., 1888; officiating presidency magistrate, March, 1886; confirmed, Nov., 1888; acted as coroner of Calcutta in 1888-89; and as inspector-general of registration in 1893 and 1894.

AMIRTA LAL CHATTERJEE, B.L., Bengal Judicial Department.—From 19th May, 1862, served in the subordinate judicial service, Bengal, and was appointed subordinate judge, 4th grade, and small cause court judge, Moorshebad, April, 1873; subordinate judge, Nuddea, Jan., 1880; 1st grade subordinate judge, April, 1880.

AMOS, ROBERT, Customs Department, Bengal.—Served in the customs department from 4th Dec., 1876, and was appointed assistant collector and head appraiser, Calcutta, July, 1882.

ANDERSON, ALEXANDER, Indian C.S. (Punjab).—Educated at Gordon's Hospital, New Grammar School, and King's College, Aberdeen; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1870; arrived in India, 4th Nov., 1872, and served in the Punjab as assistant commissioner, and forest settlement officer; on special duty, Jan.-May, 1878, and again, Nov., 1881, in the forest department; deputy commissioner, March, 1889; on special duty at Hissar, 1890-91.

ANDERSON, FRANCIS CHARLES, Indian C.S. (Central Provinces).—Educated at Edinburgh Academy, and Repton Grammar School; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1869; arrived in India, 31st Oct., 1871; from Nov., 1871, served in the Central Provinces as assistant commissioner, personal assistant to the chief commis-

sioner, and acting deputy commissioner; assistant secretary to the chief commissioner, Aug., 1881; commissioner of excise, superintendent of stamps, and inspector-general of registration, Feb., 1882; acted as secretary to chief commissioner in Oct., 1882, 1885, and March, 1888; confirmed in that appointment, Jan., 1891; officiating commissioner, Nagpur division, April, 1892, and again from Feb., 1893; commissioner, Jubbulpore, April, 1894; Nagpur, July, 1894; prepared an excise manual for the Central Provinces, 1885.

ANDERSON, FRANCIS GEOFFREY HARTNELL, Indian C.S. (Bombay).—Educated at Bath College, and Balliol College, Oxford; appointed after open competitive examination of 1892; arrived in India, 3rd Dec., 1893, and served in Bombay as assistant collector and magistrate.

ANDERSON, HENRY AIKEN, Indian C.S. (Punjab).—Educated at Edinburgh Academy, and Repton Grammar School; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1871; arrived in India, 13th Nov., 1873, and served in the Punjab as assistant commissioner, and officiating under secretary to government, March, 1880; assistant political officer, Kurram, under the government of India, Aug., 1880; on special duty, camp of exercise, Delhi, Nov., 1885; officiating district judge, in 1884, 1886, and 1887; deputy commissioner, May, 1889; acted as commissioner and superintendent, Derajat, in 1892.

ANDERSON, JAMES, M.A., Barrister-at-Law, late Bengal C.S.—Educated at Edinburgh University; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1860; arrived, 14th Nov., 1861; served in the Bengal revenue and judicial departments as assistant magistrate and collector, joint magistrate and deputy collector, and deputy commissioner, and was appointed magistrate and collector, 1st grade, March, 1884; on special duty connected with the Wahabi case, June, 1870; retired, 1888.

ANDERSON, JAMES ARTHUR, M.I.C.E., India Public Works Department.—Joined the department as assistant engineer, 13th Dec., 1871; executive engineer, Dec., 1876; employed on the Sind-Pishin, Indus Valley and Eastern Bengal railways; engineer-in-chief, Kashmir railway survey, Oct., 1890; superintendent, way and works, Eastern Bengal railway, July, 1892; superintending engineer, Oct., 1892.

ANDERSON, JAMES DRUMMOND, Indian C.S. (Bengal).—Educated at Rugby; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1873; arrived in India, 14th Nov., 1875; served in Bengal as assistant magistrate and collector, and officiating joint magistrate and deputy collector; transferred to Assam, Jan., 1880, and served as assistant commissioner and officiating deputy commissioner; assistant secretary to chief commissioner, Jan.-Dec., 1882; assistant commissioner, 1st grade, July, 1886; deputy commissioner, April, 1889; returned to Bengal as magistrate and collector, April, 1894; author of 'A Short List of Words of the Hill Tipperah Language,' 1885.

ANDERSON, JOHN ALEXANDER, B.L., Indian C.S. (Punjab).—Educated at the Grammar School and University of Aberdeen, and was also a student of the Inner Temple; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1872; arrived in India, 2nd Dec., 1874, and served in the Punjab as assistant com-

missioner; officiating divisional judge, March, 1888, and again in 1889, 1891 and 1892; district judge, April, 1889.

ANDERSON, JOHN W. M., Colonel, late Revenue Survey Department, Bombay.—First commission, Jan., 1855; from Nov., 1863, served in the revenue survey department, Mysore, as assistant superintendent and deputy superintendent; from April, 1881, served as superintendent of the Sind settlement survey; 1st grade superintendent, Jan., 1885; retired, March, 1888.

ANDERSON, ROBERT HILDEBRAND, Indian C.S. (Bengal).—Appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1874; arrived in India, 7th Dec., 1876; served as assistant magistrate and collector, officiating embarkation agent and superintendent of emigration, and officiating joint magistrate and deputy collector; confirmed as joint magistrate and deputy collector, Aug., 1887; district and sessions judge, Oct., 1893.

ANDREW, JAMES, Indian C.S. (Madras).—Educated at the Royal School, Armagh, and Kingstown School; appointed to the Madras civil service after open competitive examination of 1877; arrived in India, 17th Nov., 1879, and served in Madras as assistant collector and magistrate, acting sub-collector and joint magistrate, and forest settlement officer; head assistant to collector and magistrate, N. Arcot, April, 1888; principal assistant to collector, district magistrate, and agent, Ganjam, Dec., 1891.

ANDREWS, WILLIAM, Accounts Department, India Office.—Clerk, 2nd class, 30th March, 1876.

ANLEY, GEORGE AUGUSTUS D'AUVERGNE, late Public Works Department, Bengal.—Appointed by the government of India as executive engineer, 4th class, 25th Jan., 1861; served in the Bengal public works department, and temporarily under the municipality of Calcutta; officiating assistant to chief engineer and assistant secretary to the government of Bengal, April, 1876; held charge of office of chief engineer, and secretary to government of Bengal, Dec., 1878; superintending engineer, 3rd class, March, 1882; 2nd class, March, 1886; retired, Nov., 1888.

ANNESLEY, ROBERT, Major, Judicial Department, N.W. Provinces.—Entered the army in 1855, and served in the Crimean and Abyssinian campaigns; retired in 1869; joined the service, 4th Jan., 1871, and served as assistant district superintendent of police and officiating cantonment magistrate; confirmed in the latter appointment, June, 1877; judge, small cause court, June, 1880; 1st class cantonment magistrate, Oct., 1886.

ANSON, GEORGE WEMYSS, Lieut.-Col., I.S.C., Oudh Commission.—1st commission, 11th Jan., 1867; private secretary and A.D.C. to lieut.-governor, N.W. Provinces, May, 1871, and Feb., 1875, to June, 1878; from April, 1872, served as officiating cantonment magistrate and officiating assistant commissioner; confirmed in latter appointment, Sept., 1875, and in the former, Sept., 1877; agent, Balrampur estate, June, 1886; in addition cantonment magistrate, Aug., 1887; agent and deputy commissioner, Nov., 1890.

APJOHN, JAMES HENRY, M.A., M.I.C.E., India Public Works Department.—Joined the service, 22nd Dec., 1868, as assistant engineer, and was employed on irrigation work in Bengal;

executive engineer, Sept., 1873; services lent to Calcutta port commissioners, March, 1887.

ARBUTHNOT, SIR ALEXANDER JOHN, K.C.S.I., C.I.E., late Madras C.S., Member of the Council of India.—Educated at Rugby, and Haileybury College, and appointed to the Madras civil service in 1842; arrived in Madras, Sept., 1842; served in the revenue department as assistant collector and magistrate, and also held the posts of Malayalam translator to government, secretary to the college and university boards, and deputy and acting registrar to the Sudder court; director of public instruction, March, 1855; chief secretary to government and additional member of council for making laws and regulations, Oct., 1862; member of Madras council, Oct., 1867, to Oct., 1872; temporary governor of Madras in the absence of Lord Napier, Feb.-May, 1872; retired from the Madras civil service, Oct., 1874; member of governor-general's council, June, 1875, to 1880, and acted as president of the council, Nov., 1878, and March, 1879; vice-chancellor of the university of Madras, Oct., 1871, and of the university of Calcutta, Oct., 1878; counsellor of the Empress of India, Jan., 1877; appointed member of the council of India, 1st Nov., 1887; vice-president, 1893-94; is the author of various publications on educational and other subjects in connection with India, among which are 'Reports of Selected Cases determined in the Court of Foudjari Adawlut,' 1851; 'A Compilation of Papers relating to Public Instruction in Madras,' 1855; 'Memoir of Sir Thomas Munro,' 1881; &c.

ARBUTHNOT, SIR CHARLES GEORGE, General, G.C.B., R.A.—1st commission, Royal Artillery, June, 1843; served in the Crimean campaign, 1855, and was present at the siege and fall of Sevastopol (medal and clasp, brevet of major, 5th class Medjidie, Turkish medal); deputy adjutant-general, R.A., in Bengal, March, 1873, to Sept., 1877; acted as inspector-general of ordnance, Oct., 1877, to Sept., 1878; inspector-general of artillery in India, Sept., 1878, to July, 1880, during which period he served as brigadier-general in the operations in Afghanistan (medal, K.C.B.); deputy adjutant-general, R.A.; with the headquarters of the army in England, Sept., 1880, to Aug., 1883; inspector-general of artillery in England, Sept., 1883, to April, 1885; president, ordnance committee, May, 1885, to Jan., 1886; commander-in-chief, and member of the governor's council, Bombay, Feb. to Dec., 1886; commander-in-chief, and member of council, Madras, Dec., 1886, to March, 1891; G.C.B., May, 1894.

ARBUTHNOT, ROBERT EDWARD VAUGHAN, Indian C.S. (Burma).—Educated at the Merchant Taylors' School, London, and St. John's College, Cambridge; appointed after open competitive examination of 1890; arrived, 15th Nov., 1892, and served in Burma as assistant commissioner.

ARBUTHNOTT, JOHN CAMPBELL, Indian C.S. (Assam).—Educated at Fettes College, Edinburgh; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competitive examination of 1877; arrived in India, 21st Dec., 1879; assistant magistrate and collector, Bhagalpur, Dec., 1879; transferred to Assam, Feb., 1883, and served as assistant commissioner; deputy commissioner, Nov., 1890.

ARCHER, CHARLES, Captain, I.S.C., Foreign Department, Government of India.—First commission, 9th Sept., 1882; entered civil employ, 8th Aug., 1887, as acting assistant agent, Baluchistan; confirmed, May, 1889; assistant secretary to government of India, foreign department, April, 1891; on special duty under that department, March-April, 1892; political agent in Zohar, Jan., 1894.

ARDAGH, SIR JOHN CHARLES, K.C.I.E., C.B., Colonel, R.E.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; entered royal engineers as lieutenant, 1st April, 1859; captain, Aug., 1872; major, Nov., 1878; lieutenant-colonel, Nov., 1882; colonel, Nov., 1887; attached to the special embassy during the congress at Berlin, June, 1878, and was made a C.B. (civil), July, 1878; appointed assistant to Her Majesty's commissioner for the delimitation of the frontier of Bulgaria, Sept., 1878; appointed commissioner for the demarcation of the Greek frontier, June, 1881; was employed during the conference at Berlin on the Greek frontier in 1880, and during the conference at Constantinople on the same subject in 1881; served in the Egyptian campaign of 1882-4, including the operations at Alexandria, and the battle of Tel-el-Kebir (medal with clasp, bronze star, brevet of lieutenant-colonel and 4th class of Osmanieh); served in the Soudan in 1884, commanding royal engineers and chief of the intelligence department (battles of Teb and Tamai; 2 clasps); C.B. (military), May, 1884; served in Soudan expedition, 1884-5; commandant of base at Cairo, and in the expedition of 1885-6 with the frontier field force; has received the 3rd class of the Medjidie; private secretary to the Right Hon. the Marquess of Lansdowne, governor-general of India, from Dec., 1888, to Jan., 1894; C.I.E., Jan., 1892; K.C.I.E., Jan., 1894.

ARGYLL (DUKE OF), HIS GRACE GEORGE DOUGLAS CAMPBELL, K.G., K.T., P.C.—Born in 1823; succeeded to the dukedom in 1847; took an active part in the controversy in the Presbyterian Church of Scotland relating to patronage, and published various pamphlets and essays in connection therewith, 1842-1848; elected chancellor of the University of St. Andrew's, 1851; lord privy seal, 1852; post-master general, 1855; K.T., 1856; lord privy seal, 1859; in 1860 he was again appointed postmaster general, and subsequently lord privy seal; secretary of state for India, Dec., 1868, to Feb., 1874; lord privy seal, May, 1880; resigned in April, 1881; K.G., 1884; elected rector of the University of Glasgow, Nov., 1854; presided over the 25th annual meeting of the British Association, held at Glasgow, Sept., 1855; president of the Royal Society of Edinburgh, 1861; is hereditary master of the Queen's household in Scotland, a trustee of the British Museum, and hereditary sheriff and lord lieutenant of Argyllshire; published 'The Reign of Law' in 1866; 'Primeval Man,' 1869; 'a small work on the history and antiquities of Iona,' 1870; 'The Patronage Act of 1874,' 1874; observations 'On the Important Questions involved in the relation of Landlord and Tenant,' 1877; 'The Eastern Question from the Treaty of Paris to the Treaty of Berlin, and to the second Afghan War'; &c.

ARMSTRONG, FRANCIS BUSFIELD, Financial Department, India Office.—Educated at Cheltenham Proprietary College, and King Edward's

School Birmingham; junior clerk, financial department, India office, 30th March, 1869; senior clerk, Sept., 1883.

ARMSTRONG, JOHN ARTHUR, Lieut.-Col., R.E., late Public Works Department, Punjab.—1st commission, June, 1860; joined the public works department, Nov., 1865, and served in the Punjab as assistant engineer; served in the Hazara campaign, 1868, as assistant engineer in charge of field telegraph; executive engineer, Sept., 1869; assistant to chief engineer, and assistant secretary to government, April, 1877; served in Kashmir on special road work, 1881-82; superintending engineer, 3rd class, March, 1886; retired, March, 1887.

ARMSTRONG, JOHN SCOBELL, late Bengal C.S.—Educated at Marlborough College; appointed to the Bengal civil service after open competition of 1864; arrived, 3rd March, 1866; served in the revenue and judicial departments in Bengal; deputy collector of customs, Calcutta, Dec., 1877; magistrate and collector, 2nd grade, and collector of customs, Calcutta, April, 1883; port commissioner, Calcutta, Nov., 1887; retired, July, 1892.

ARNOLD, GEORGE FREDERICK, B.A., Indian C.S. (Burma).—Educated at Merchant Taylors School, and Queen's College, Oxford; appointed after open competitive examination of 1892; arrived in India, 1st Jan., 1894, and served in Burma as assistant commissioner.
